Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: OP3 International Pte Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: OP3 International Pte Ltd was incorporated on 20 December 2006 to engage in interior design, decorating consultancy and construction activities.
From 1 August 2010, OP3 International Pte Ltd had Mr Foo Kian Beng as its sole director and he was employed as its managing director.

On 19 July 2013, OP3 International Pte Ltd entered into an agreement with Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd under which OP3 was to provide fitting out works, including design and construction, at Smile Inc's clinic at Suntec City Mall for a contract price of S$158,010.
On 9 January 2014, mould was discovered on the walls of Smile Inc's clinic at Suntec City Mall following a flood in the clinic.
By 17 January 2014, OP3 International Pte Ltd carried out and completed remedial and rectification works at Smile Inc's clinic following the first flood.
On 21 July 2014, mould was again discovered on the walls of Smile Inc's clinic at Suntec City Mall following a second flood in the clinic, and rectification works were carried out by a third party.
On 10 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd passed a resolution declaring S$1,200,000 in dividends for the financial year 2012 in favour of Mr Foo Kian Beng.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid S$1,200,000 in dividends for the financial year 2012 to Mr Foo Kian Beng.
On 10 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd passed a resolution declaring S$700,000 in dividends for the financial year 2014 in favour of Mr Foo Kian Beng.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid S$700,000 in dividends for the financial year 2014 to Mr Foo Kian Beng.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd repaid S$138,352 to Mr Foo Kian Beng as moneys owing to him.
As at 31 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd's financial statements recorded net assets of S$1,136,982 and cash and cash equivalents of S$1,551,604 including a bank balance of S$1,289,216.30.
As at 31 December 2015, invoices due and payable by OP3 International Pte Ltd totalled S$941,224.81, with additional invoices amounting to S$456,349.12.
On 13 July 2016, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid S$400,000 in dividends for the financial year 2015 to Mr Foo Kian Beng.
On 27 December 2016, OP3 International Pte Ltd resolved to declare S$500,000 in dividends for 2015.
As at 31 December 2016, OP3 International Pte Ltd's financial statements recorded net assets of S$157,683 and a net loss for the year of S$72,299.
In 2017, OP3 International Pte Ltd made repayments totalling S$682,394 to Mr Foo Kian Beng in respect of amounts recorded as due to him.
For the financial year ended 31 December 2016, OP3 International Pte Ltd recorded revenue of S$1,343,323, representing an 87.6% decrease from its 2015 revenue.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, given OP3 International Pte Ltd's financial position and the contingent exposure from a substantial construction claim, the declaration and payment of dividends and the repayment of moneys to its director were consistent with the director's duty to consider creditor interests.
Party_Details.Rule: Under Singapore company law, when a company is insolvent or in a financially precarious condition, directors must take into account the interests of creditors when deciding on transactions that distribute or apply company assets, including dividends and repayments to insiders.
Party_Details.Application: OP3 International Pte Ltd entered into a contract in July 2013 and, after flooding incidents at the project site in 2014, faced a potential liability of approximately S$1.8 million. In December 2015, the company declared and paid dividends totalling S$1,900,000 to its sole director. In 2016, the company paid a further S$400,000 and resolved to pay another S$500,000 in dividends during a period of sharp revenue decline. In 2017, the company repaid S$682,394 to its director while experiencing significant financial distress. These transactions reduced the company's asset pool during the period when the construction claim remained unresolved.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge analysed when the director's duty to consider creditor interests arises, holding that it is triggered when a company is financially parlous, not only when it is on the verge of insolvency. The court rejected the use of hindsight to assess contingent liabilities and applied the test of whether a contingent liability was reasonably likely to materialise based on information available at the material time. For HC/S 498/2015, the court found that as at 25 May 2015 some liability was reasonably likely to materialise and that a contingent liability in the range of S$441,000 to S$514,500 should have been taken into account. The court rejected the plaintiff's higher figures of S$1,542,206 and S$534,189 as the appropriate contingent values and also rejected the defendant's position that no provision was needed. On solvency, the court held that OP3 was solvent and not financially parlous as at 31 December 2015, so the December 2015 dividends and the S$138,352 repayment did not involve a breach of duty. However, the court concluded that OP3 was insolvent on a balance sheet basis by 31 December 2016 and clearly insolvent by 31 December 2017. In relation to the S$500,000 dividend declared on 27 December 2016, the court held that by that time Foo Kian Beng was required to consider creditor interests. The court found that his reliance on legal advice from Parwani Law LLC and on the expert report of Dr Chee Yan Pong was insufficient to justify treating the HC/S 498/2015 claim as having no meaningful likelihood of liability, and that his informal consultation with auditor Wong King Kheng was based on the incorrect assumption that no provision for the contingent liability was needed. The court therefore held that causing OP3 to declare and pay the S$500,000 dividend in these circumstances was not consistent with acting bona fide in the interests of the company and its creditors. Regarding the S$682,394 repaid in 2017, the court rejected Foo Kian Beng's argument that this amount included the S$500,000 dividend and that there was double counting, preferring the audited 2016 and 2017 cash flow statements which treated the S$500,000 dividend and the S$682,394 repayment as separate items. Given OP3's insolvency by the end of 2016, the court held that causing OP3 to repay S$682,394 to him in 2017, without any legitimate justification for preferring his position over other creditors, amounted to a misapplication of company assets to the prejudice of creditors and a breach of his duty to act in the company's and creditors' interests. The court rejected the allegation of diversion of business to OP3 Creative Pte Ltd, noting the absence of specific diverted contracts or identified opportunities and finding that the mere association of email and website details and the drop in revenue were insufficient to prove diversion. On relief under section 391 of the Companies Act, the court held that Foo Kian Beng had not shown that he acted both honestly and reasonably in relation to the impugned dividend and repayment transactions. OP3's claim was therefore allowed in part, limited to the S$500,000 dividend declared in late 2016 and the S$682,394 repayment in 2017, with the remainder of the claims dismissed.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Foo Kian Beng

Facts.Text: From 1 August 2010, Foo Kian Beng was the sole director of OP3 International Pte Ltd and was employed as its managing director.

On 4 May 2010, OP3 Creative Pte Ltd was incorporated to carry out activities including manufacturing of carpentry and other construction works, and Foo Kian Beng was its sole director and shareholder.

On 19 July 2013, as director of OP3 International Pte Ltd, Foo Kian Beng caused OP3 to enter into a contract with Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd for fitting out works at Smile Inc's clinic at Suntec City Mall.
By 17 January 2014, under the direction of Foo Kian Beng, OP3 International Pte Ltd carried out and completed remedial and rectification works at Smile Inc's clinic following the first flood.
On 10 December 2015, Foo Kian Beng approved a resolution for OP3 International Pte Ltd to declare S$1,200,000 in dividends for the financial year 2012 in his favour.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid S$1,200,000 in dividends for the financial year 2012 to Foo Kian Beng.
On 10 December 2015, Foo Kian Beng approved a resolution for OP3 International Pte Ltd to declare S$700,000 in dividends for the financial year 2014 in his favour.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid S$700,000 in dividends for the financial year 2014 to Foo Kian Beng.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd repaid S$138,352 to Foo Kian Beng in respect of amounts recorded as owing to him.
On 8 July 2016, Foo Kian Beng approved a resolution for OP3 International Pte Ltd to declare S$400,000 in dividends for the financial year 2015 in his favour.
On 13 July 2016, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid S$400,000 in dividends for the financial year 2015 to Foo Kian Beng.
On 27 December 2016, Foo Kian Beng approved a resolution for OP3 International Pte Ltd to declare S$500,000 in dividends for the financial year 2015 in his favour.
In 2017, OP3 International Pte Ltd made repayments totalling S$682,394 to Foo Kian Beng in respect of amounts recorded as due to him.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the decisions by Foo Kian Beng to cause the company to declare dividends and repay amounts to him while a substantial construction claim was outstanding were consistent with director duties.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors must act in the interests of the company, and when the company is in financial difficulty, this includes taking creditor interests into account when authorising distributions or repayments to themselves.
Party_Details.Application: Foo Kian Beng was the sole director and managing director of the company. Between 2015 and 2017, he authorized a sequence of dividend payments and debt repayments to himself. These transactions occurred while the company faced a construction dispute claiming S$1.8 million and experiencing a sharp decline in revenue and net assets. The timing of these payments reduced the company's available capital during a period of increasing financial instability.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court accepted that Foo Kian Beng was the sole director and managing director of OP3 International Pte Ltd from 1 August 2010 to 3 April 2020 and that he controlled the impugned transactions. The judge held that the duty to consider creditor interests arises when a company is financially parlous, not only when it is technically insolvent or on the verge of insolvency. Applying this framework, the court examined OP3's financial position at the end of 2015, 2016 and 2017 and the contingent liability from HC/S 498/2015. The court found that as at 25 May 2015, some liability from HC/S 498/2015 was reasonably likely to materialise and that an objective assessment required a contingent liability in the range of S$441,000 to S$514,500 to be recognised. The court rejected Foo Kian Beng's primary position that no contingent liability should be recognised and his reliance on accounting standards to avoid provision. On solvency, the court held that OP3 was solvent and not financially parlous as at 31 December 2015, so the December 2015 dividends and the S$138,352 repayment did not involve a breach of duty. However, the court concluded that OP3 was insolvent on a balance sheet basis by 31 December 2016 and clearly insolvent by 31 December 2017. In relation to the S$500,000 dividend declared on 27 December 2016, the court held that by that time Foo Kian Beng was required to consider creditor interests. The court found that his reliance on legal advice from Parwani Law LLC and on the expert report of Dr Chee Yan Pong was insufficient to justify treating the HC/S 498/2015 claim as having no meaningful likelihood of liability, and that his informal consultation with auditor Wong King Kheng was based on the incorrect assumption that no provision for the contingent liability was needed. The court therefore held that causing OP3 to declare and pay the S$500,000 dividend in these circumstances was not consistent with acting bona fide in the interests of the company and its creditors. Regarding the S$682,394 repaid in 2017, the court rejected Foo Kian Beng's argument that this amount included the S$500,000 dividend and that there was double counting, preferring the audited 2016 and 2017 cash flow statements which treated the S$500,000 dividend and the S$682,394 repayment as separate items. Given OP3's insolvency by the end of 2016, the court held that causing OP3 to repay S$682,394 to him in 2017, without any legitimate justification for preferring his position over other creditors, amounted to a misapplication of company assets to the prejudice of creditors and a breach of his duty to act in the company's and creditors' interests. The court rejected the allegation of diversion of business to OP3 Creative Pte Ltd, noting the absence of specific diverted contracts or identified opportunities and finding that the mere association of email and website details and the drop in revenue were insufficient to prove diversion. On relief under section 391 of the Companies Act, the court held that Foo Kian Beng had not shown that he acted both honestly and reasonably in relation to the impugned dividend and repayment transactions. OP3's claim was therefore allowed in part, limited to the S$500,000 dividend declared in late 2016 and the S$682,394 repayment in 2017, with the remainder of the claims dismissed.
